attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start the refactor phase by reading the architecture notes and the current state of the code.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md

example-mapping
metrics.json
node_modules
package.json

run.log
src

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: tightened `runScenario` return type from `{ results: unknown[] }` to `{ results: { premium: number }[] }`
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — documents the shape tests exercise; `premium: number` field-name contract is now visible at the signature
6	- Naming established: `runScenario(scenario) -> { results: { premium: number }[] }`; field name `premium` confirmed (per example-mapping contract)
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: extracted magic numbers in `runScenario` to module-level named constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1` and `PROCESSING_FEE = 5`
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — these are global pricing-rule constants that more upcoming tests will reference; naming them centralises and documents intent at every use site. Rule 2 trumps APP (mass +5).
11	- Naming established: `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` (10% surcharge on first-insurance, applied to policy base), `PROCESSING_FEE` (flat 5 G added to every quote), `BASE_PREMIUM` (lookup table by item type); per-step local names `policyBase` (sum of item base premiums) and `firstInsurance` (surcharge amount, not boolean flag)
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: extracted per-step pricing into a private module-level function `priceQuoteStep(step: QuoteStep) -> { premium: number }`; `runScenario` is now just `scenario.steps.map(priceQuoteStep)`
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — names the "price one quote step" concept that was previously anonymous inside `.map(...)`. Upcoming surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty, follow-up) have a clear home and won't bloat `runScenario`. Mass roughly neutral.
16	- Naming established: `priceQuoteStep(step)` is the canonical per-step pricing helper; future surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty discount, follow-up discount) should compose inside this function, not in `runScenario`
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- Change: extracted `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` helpers; named the `0.5` magic number as `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`; renamed local `curseSurcharge` to `itemSurchargeTotal` (now an accumulator for all per-item surcharges, not just curse).
20	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` lookup was duplicated across two reduces; now centralised in `itemBasePremium`. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `itemSurcharges(item)` is the natural extension point for upcoming high-enchantment (per-item) surcharge.
21	- Naming established: `itemBasePremium(item) -> number` (single source of base-premium lookup, will absorb rune-block logic later); `itemSurcharges(item) -> number` (sum of all per-item surcharges — add high-enchantment here next); `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5`; the local accumulator name `itemSurchargeTotal` (NOT `curseSurcharge`) reflects that it sums all per-item surcharges.
22

content.text:
All 5 tests still pass. Task tracking is already established by orchestrator. Not adjusting it from refactor scope.

## Step 5: Calculate Refactored APP Mass

New `itemSurcharges`:
- Constants: 2 (two `0` literals) = 2
- Bindings: 5 (item, base, curseSurcharge, highEnchantmentSurcharge, item.cursed, item.enchantment) = ~6
- Invocations: 5 (itemBasePremium, `*` x2, `??`, `>=`) = 10
- Conditionals: 2 (two ternaries) = 8
- Loops: 0 = 0
- Assignments: 0 = 0

Total mass: ~26 (down from ~43)

Mass reduction: 43 → 26 (Δ -17), driven by eliminating 3 reassignments (-18 mass).

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) + Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Replaced the imperative `let surcharge = 0; if (…) surcharge += …` accumulator with two named expression bindings (`curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge`) summed in the return.
- Mass: ~43 → ~26 (Δ -17), driven by removing three assignments (each costing 6).
- Naming: Each surcharge component is now visibly named at the call site, making it self-evident which surcharges contribute and how they compose. Tests stay green.

## Step 8: Append to Architecture Notes

## After cycle 4
- Change: extracted `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` helpers; named the `0.5` magic number as `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`; renamed local `curseSurcharge` to `itemSurchargeTotal` (now an accumulator for all per-item surcharges, not just curse).
- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` lookup was duplicated across two reduces; now centralised in `itemBasePremium`. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `itemSurcharges(item)` is the natural extension point for upcoming high-enchantment (per-item) surcharge.
- Naming established: `itemBasePremium(item) -> number` (single source of base-premium lookup, will absorb rune-block logic later); `itemSurcharges(item) -> number` (sum of all per-item surcharges — add high-enchantment here next); `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5`; the local accumulator name `itemSurchargeTotal` (NOT `curseSurcharge`) reflects that it sums all per-item surcharges.
input.new_string:
## After cycle 4
- Change: extracted `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` helpers; named the `0.5` magic number as `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`; renamed local `curseSurcharge` to `itemSurchargeTotal` (now an accumulator for all per-item surcharges, not just curse).
- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` lookup was duplicated across two reduces; now centralised in `itemBasePremium`. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `itemSurcharges(item)` is the natural extension point for upcoming high-enchantment (per-item) surcharge.
- Naming established: `itemBasePremium(item) -> number` (single source of base-premium lookup, will absorb rune-block logic later); `itemSurcharges(item) -> number` (sum of all per-item surcharges — add high-enchantment here next); `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5`; the local accumulator name `itemSurchargeTotal` (NOT `curseSurcharge`) reflects that it sums all per-item surcharges.

## After cycle 5
- Change: rewrote `itemSurcharges` from imperative `let surcharge = 0; if (…) surcharge += …` accumulator to two named expression bindings (`curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge`) summed in the return; added constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — eliminated 3 assignments and a mutable `let`, reducing APP mass ~43 → ~26 (Δ -17). Rule 2 (reveals intent) — each surcharge component is now a named value at the call site, so future surcharges added inside `itemSurcharges` follow a clear pattern: `const xSurcharge = condition ? base * X_RATE : 0`.
- Naming established: pattern for new per-item surcharges is a named binding `<name>Surcharge = condition ? base * <NAME>_RATE : 0`; the return is a flat sum of these bindings (no mutable accumulator). Constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.

content.text:
## Step 7: Report

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Rewrote `itemSurcharges` from imperative `let` + `if … +=` accumulator to two named expression bindings (`curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge`) summed in the return. Each surcharge component is now visibly named, and the function is purely expression-based.
- **Mass Change**: ~43 → ~26 (Δ -17), driven by eliminating 3 assignments. Conditional count unchanged (2 ternaries replace 2 `if`s).
- **Simple Design rules applied**: Rule 4 (fewest elements — removed mutable state) and Rule 2 (reveals intent — each surcharge has a name at the call site).
- **Tests**: All 5 passing.

Proceeding to the next test.
